fragments.content: Renal dosing adjusts drug regimens based on kidney function to prevent toxicity from drug accumulation. Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) determines clearance of renally eliminated drugs. Estimated GFR (eGFR) calculated from serum creatinine using CKD-EPI or MDRD equations; Cockcroft-Gault formula estimates creatinine clearance (CrCl) for drug dosing. Normal GFR: 90-120 mL/min. Mild impairment: 60-89; Moderate: 30-59; Severe: 15-29; Kidney failure: <15. Drugs requiring renal adjustment: aminoglycosides, vancomycin, digoxin, lithium, gabapentin, direct oral anticoagulants (except apixaban), metformin (contraindicated if eGFR <30). Some drugs are safe regardless of renal function due to hepatic metabolism.

Renal dose adjustment strategies: reduce individual dose while maintaining frequency (gentamicin); extend dosing interval while maintaining dose (vancomycin); or both. CrCl-based dosing guidelines available in prescribing information. For CrCl 30-50: typically 50-75% of normal dose; CrCl 10-30: 25-50% of normal dose; CrCl <10: avoid or use minimal doses with monitoring. Dialysis considerations: schedule post-dialysis dosing for dialyzable drugs (aminoglycosides, vancomycin); supplemental doses needed after dialysis. Avoid nephrotoxic combinations (NSAIDs + ACE inhibitors + diuretics = triple whammy). Metformin: reduce dose if eGFR 30-45; contraindicated <30. DOACs: apixaban safest in CKD; avoid dabigatran if CrCl <30.

Creatinine clearance estimation: Cockcroft-Gault equation: CrCl = [(140-age) × weight(kg)] / (72 × SCr) × 0.85 (if female). Use actual body weight unless obese (>120% ideal body weight), then use adjusted body weight. CKD-EPI equation more accurate for eGFR >60. Drug dosing studies historically used Cockcroft-Gault; FDA now accepts CKD-EPI. Therapeutic drug monitoring essential for narrow therapeutic index renally-cleared drugs: vancomycin trough 10-20 mcg/mL; gentamicin peak 6-10, trough <2 mcg/mL; digoxin 0.5-0.9 ng/mL; lithium 0.6-1.2 mEq/L. Acute kidney injury requires daily reassessment of renal function and dose adjustment. Augmented renal clearance (CrCl >130) in critically ill may require higher doses.
